Algeria: Algeria have appeared in the tournament 5 times (1982, 1986, 2010, 2014, 2026), with their best finish being the round of 16 in 2014.
In 1982, they famously defeated West Germany 2-1 in one of the greatest upsets in tournament history, only to be eliminated by the controversial 'Disgrace of Gijon' match.
Rabah Madjer, Lakhdar Belloumi, and Islam Slimani are among the most notable Algerian players to feature in the competition.
The 2014 squad, led by coach Vahid Halilhodzic, came agonizingly close to the quarterfinals, losing 2-1 to Germany in extra time.
Argentina: Argentina have appeared in the tournament 19 times and won it 3 times (1978, 1986, 2022), placing them among the most successful nations in the history of the event.
Diego Maradona's legendary performances in 1986, including the 'Hand of God' and 'Goal of the Century' against England, remain the most iconic individual tournament moments ever.
Argentina hosted and won the tournament in 1978, defeating the Netherlands 3-1 in the final in Buenos Aires.
Lionel Messi, widely regarded as one of the greatest players of all time, finally lifted the trophy in 2022 in Qatar after four previous attempts spanning 16 years.
Mario Kempes (1978) and Guillermo Stabile (1930) are among Argentina's all-time top scorers in the competition.
Australia: Australia have appeared in the tournament 6 times (2006, 2010, 2014, 2018, 2022, 2026), with their best result being the round of 16 in 2006 and 2022.
Tim Cahill is the Socceroos' greatest ever tournament player, scoring memorable goals including a stunning volley against the Netherlands in 2014.
Australia qualified through the Asian confederation starting in 2006 after decades of near-misses while competing in the Oceanian zone.
Their 2022 run to the round of 16 in Qatar, where they defeated Denmark and Tunisia, was celebrated as one of Australian football's finest achievements.
Austria: Austria have appeared in the tournament 8 times, with their best finish being third place in 1954.
The 1954 squad achieved a remarkable 7-5 victory over Switzerland in the quarterfinals, which remains the highest-scoring match in tournament history.
Austria co-hosted the European Championship in 2008 but have never hosted the tournament.
Legendary midfielder Ernst Ocwirk and modern star David Alaba are among Austria's most celebrated footballers to feature in international competition.
Belgium: Belgium have appeared in the tournament 14 times, with their best finish being third place in 2018 when their 'Golden Generation' defeated England in the third-place match.
Belgium's squad of the 2010s and 2020s, featuring Eden Hazard, Kevin De Bruyne, and Romelu Lukaku, was widely regarded as one of the strongest in the world.
Belgium reached the semifinals in 1986 under coach Guy Thys, defeating the Soviet Union and Spain en route before falling to Argentina and Maradona.
They were among the original 13 teams to participate in the inaugural 1930 tournament in Uruguay.
Bosnia and Herzegovina: Bosnia and Herzegovina made their tournament debut in 2014 in Brazil, marking a historic milestone for the young nation that gained independence in 1992.
Despite being eliminated in the group stage in 2014, they secured a memorable 3-1 victory over Iran, their first-ever tournament win.
Edin Dzeko, the country's all-time leading scorer, spearheaded the qualification campaign and scored in the 2014 tournament.
Their 2026 appearance marks only their second time qualifying for the event, making them one of the tournament's newest participants.
Brazil: Brazil are the most successful team in tournament history with 5 titles (1958, 1962, 1970, 1994, 2002) and have appeared in every single edition of the event, a record unmatched by any other nation.
The 1970 squad featuring Pele, Jairzinho, Rivelino, and Tostao is widely considered the greatest team ever assembled in the history of the sport.

Brazil hosted the tournament twice, in 1950 and 2014, but suffered heartbreak both times -- losing the 1950 final to Uruguay in the 'Maracanazo' and being demolished 7-1 by Germany in the 2014 semifinals.
Ronaldo's 15 tournament goals make him one of the competition's all-time leading scorers, including both goals in the 2002 final against Germany.
Pele remains the only player to win the tournament three times (1958, 1962, 1970) and scored 12 goals across four editions.
Cabo Verde: Cabo Verde's qualification for Football 2026 represents their first-ever appearance in the tournament, a remarkable achievement for a small island nation of roughly 600,000 people.
The Cape Verdean national team, nicknamed the 'Blue Sharks,' have risen steadily in African football, reaching the Africa Cup of Nations quarterfinals in 2013 and 2023.
Many of their players come from the large Cape Verdean diaspora, with squad members born in Portugal, the Netherlands, France, and other European countries.
Their qualification campaign saw them overcome much larger African nations, cementing their status as one of the great underdog stories heading into the 2026 competition.
Canada: Canada have appeared in the tournament 3 times (1986, 2022, 2026) and will co-host Football 2026 alongside the USA and Mexico.
In their first appearance in 1986, Canada failed to score a goal in their three group matches, but their 2022 return to the event after 36 years was celebrated across the country.
Alphonso Davies, the Bayern Munich star, has become the face of Canadian football and led the team's resurgence in the 2020s.
As co-hosts of Football 2026, Canada will stage matches in Vancouver and Toronto, marking the first time the country has hosted tournament matches.
Colombia: Colombia have appeared in the tournament 7 times, with their best finish being the quarterfinals in 2014 when James Rodriguez won the Golden Boot with 6 goals.
Carlos Valderrama, with his iconic blond afro, became one of the most recognizable players in tournament history during his appearances in 1990, 1994, and 1998.
The 1994 tournament was marred by tragedy when defender Andres Escobar was murdered after returning home following an own goal against the USA.
Colombia's 5-0 demolition of Argentina in a 1993 qualifying match remains one of the most stunning results in South American football history.
Congo DR: Congo DR (formerly Zaire) made tournament history in 1974 as the first sub-Saharan African team to qualify for the event, though they lost all three group matches.
Their Football 2026 appearance ends a 52-year wait to return to the competition, making it one of the longest gaps between tournament appearances.
The 1974 squad is remembered for Mwepu Ilunga's infamous free-kick incident against Brazil, where he ran out of the wall and kicked the ball away before it was taken.
Modern stars like Chancel Mbemba and Cedric Bakambu have helped revive Congolese football on the international stage.
Croatia: Croatia have appeared in the tournament 7 times since their debut in 1998 and achieved a remarkable runner-up finish in both 2018 and 2022.
Their 1998 debut was sensational, with Davor Suker winning the Golden Boot as Croatia finished third, defeating Germany 3-0 in the quarterfinals.
Luka Modric, winner of the 2018 Golden Ball as the tournament's best player, led Croatia to back-to-back finals in a historic achievement for a nation of only 4 million people.
Croatia's 2022 semifinal run included a dramatic penalty shootout victory over Brazil, one of the most memorable upsets in recent tournament history.
Curaçao: Curaçao's qualification for Football 2026 marks their first-ever appearance in the tournament, a historic achievement for the small Caribbean island of approximately 150,000 people.
The team represents one of the constituent countries of the Kingdom of the Netherlands and draws heavily on players of Curaçaoan descent from the Dutch Eredivisie.
Their successful qualifying campaign through the CONCACAF pathway cemented them as one of the most exciting newcomer stories heading into Football 2026.
Curaçao's rise in Caribbean football accelerated in the 2010s, with notable performances in the Gold Cup and Nations League competitions.
Czechia: Czechia (formerly Czechoslovakia) have a rich tournament history spanning 10 appearances, including runner-up finishes in 1934 and 1962 as Czechoslovakia.
As an independent nation since 1993, the Czech Republic qualified for the 2006 tournament in Germany with a squad led by Pavel Nedved and Jan Koller.
Czechoslovakia's 1934 squad reached the final in Italy, losing 2-1 to the hosts, and repeated the feat in 1962 in Chile, falling to Brazil.
Josef Masopust won the Ballon d'Or in 1962 and scored in the final against Brazil, cementing his place as one of the greatest Czech players in tournament history.
Côte d'Ivoire: Côte d'Ivoire have appeared in the tournament 4 times (2006, 2010, 2014, 2026), with their best finish being the group stage.
Didier Drogba, one of Africa's greatest-ever players, led the Elephants in their first three tournament appearances and famously helped unite a war-torn nation through football.
The 'Golden Generation' of the 2000s and 2010s featured stars like Yaya Toure, Kolo Toure, and Gervinho, though they never advanced past the group stage.
Their 2023 Africa Cup of Nations triumph on home soil as hosts reignited the nation's football passion heading into Football 2026.
Ecuador: Ecuador have appeared in the tournament 5 times (2002, 2006, 2014, 2022, 2026), with their best finish being the round of 16 in 2006.
Their 2002 debut in Korea/Japan was a historic moment for Ecuadorian football, breaking through after decades of failed qualifying attempts.
Enner Valencia is Ecuador's all-time leading tournament scorer, netting crucial goals in both the 2014 and 2022 editions.
The altitude of Quito (2,850 meters above sea level) has been a significant home advantage in South American qualifying, making Ecuador's home ground one of the most feared venues on the continent.
Egypt: Egypt have appeared in the tournament 4 times (1934, 1990, 2018, 2026), with long gaps between their early and modern appearances.
Egypt were the first African team to appear in the tournament, participating in the 1934 edition in Italy.
Mohamed Salah, one of the world's best players, led Egypt to the 2018 tournament in Russia but was hampered by a shoulder injury sustained in the Champions League final weeks earlier.
Despite being the most successful team in Africa Cup of Nations history with 7 titles, Egypt have struggled to translate that dominance to the global stage.
England: England have appeared in the tournament 17 times and won it once, in 1966 on home soil at Wembley, defeating West Germany 4-2 in the final.

The 1966 triumph remains England's only major tournament victory, with Geoff Hurst's hat-trick in the final -- including the controversial 'Wembley Goal' -- among the most debated moments in football history.
Gary Lineker (10 goals, including the 1986 Golden Boot), Wayne Rooney, and Harry Kane are among England's most prolific scorers in the competition.
England have suffered heartbreaking penalty shootout eliminations in the tournament in 1990, 1998, and 2006, making spot-kicks a source of national anxiety.
Their run to the 2018 semifinals under Gareth Southgate ended a 28-year wait to reach that stage of the competition.
France: France have appeared in the tournament 17 times and won it twice (1998, 2018), finishing as runners-up in 2006 and 2022.
France hosted and won the 1998 tournament with a dominant squad led by Zinedine Zidane, who scored twice in the 3-0 final victory over Brazil.
Kylian Mbappe announced himself on the world stage in 2018 at age 19 and then scored a hat-trick in the 2022 final, though France lost on penalties to Argentina.
Just Fontaine's 13 goals in the 1958 tournament remain the all-time record for a single edition, a mark that has stood for nearly 70 years.
Zidane's headbutt on Marco Materazzi in the 2006 final, his last professional match, is one of the most infamous moments in tournament history.
Germany: Germany have appeared in the tournament 20 times and won it 4 times (1954, 1974, 1990, 2014), making them one of the most successful nations in the history of the event.
Germany hosted the tournament twice (1974 and 2006), winning the title on home soil in 1974 with a squad led by Franz Beckenbauer and Gerd Muller.
Miroslav Klose holds the all-time tournament scoring record with 16 goals across four editions (2002-2014), surpassing Ronaldo's previous mark in the 2014 semifinals.
The 2014 squad's 7-1 demolition of Brazil in the semifinals in Belo Horizonte is one of the most shocking results in tournament history.
Germany were eliminated in the group stage in both 2018 and 2022, marking an unprecedented decline for such a historically dominant nation.
Ghana: Ghana have appeared in the tournament 5 times (2006, 2010, 2014, 2022, 2026), with their best finish being the quarterfinals in 2010.

The 2010 quarterfinal against Uruguay produced one of the most dramatic moments in tournament history, when Luis Suarez's deliberate handball on the line denied Ghana a winning goal, and Asamoah Gyan missed the resulting penalty.
Ghana became the third African team to reach the quarterfinals, following Cameroon (1990) and Senegal (2002), and came closest of all African nations to reaching the semifinals.
Asamoah Gyan is Ghana's all-time leading tournament scorer, and the Black Stars' exciting style of play has made them a fan favorite at the event.
Haiti: Haiti's qualification for Football 2026 marks their return to the tournament for the first time since 1974, a gap of 52 years.

In 1974, Haiti became the first Caribbean nation to qualify for the tournament, and Emmanuel Sanon scored a famous goal against Italy, ending goalkeeper Dino Zoff's record 1,142-minute international unbeaten streak.
The Haitian national team's resurgence is a source of immense pride for a nation that has endured devastating natural disasters and political instability.
Haiti's qualification is one of the great underdog stories of the CONCACAF qualifying campaign for Football 2026.
IR Iran: Iran have appeared in the tournament 7 times (1978, 1998, 2006, 2014, 2018, 2022, 2026), making them the most frequent Asian qualifier alongside Japan and Korea Republic.
Their most famous tournament moment came in 1998 when they defeated the USA 2-1 in a politically charged group-stage match in Lyon, France.
Ali Daei, who held the all-time international goal-scoring record for over 15 years, represented Iran in the 1998 and 2006 tournaments.
Iran's disciplined defensive approach has made them difficult opponents; they held Portugal to a draw and nearly knocked them out in the 2018 group stage.
Iraq: Iraq have appeared in the tournament only once before, in 1986 in Mexico, where they lost all three group matches.
Their qualification for Football 2026 ends a 40-year absence from the competition and represents a remarkable achievement given the country's decades of conflict and instability.
Iraq won the 2007 Asian Cup in a stunning run that united a nation torn apart by war, though they were unable to convert that success into tournament qualification at the time.
The Iraqi national team's resilience and perseverance through extraordinary adversity has made them one of football's most inspiring stories.
Japan: Japan have appeared in the tournament 8 times consecutively since their debut in 1998, with their best finish being the round of 16 (2002, 2010, 2018, 2022).
Japan co-hosted the 2002 tournament with Korea Republic, marking the first time the event was held in Asia and the first time it was co-hosted by two nations.
In 2022, Japan stunned the world by defeating both Germany and Spain in the group stage, earning the nickname 'giant killers' before losing to Croatia on penalties.
Japanese fans have become famous worldwide for cleaning up stadiums after matches, setting an example of sportsmanship that has been widely celebrated.
Keisuke Honda, Shinji Kagawa, and Takumi Minamino are among the Japanese stars who have made their mark on the tournament stage.
Jordan: Jordan's qualification for Football 2026 marks their first-ever appearance in the tournament, a historic milestone for the Middle Eastern nation.
The Jordanian national team reached the final of the 2023 Asian Cup, losing to Qatar, which demonstrated their rapid rise in Asian football.
Jordan's success has been built on a combination of domestic development and diaspora players, creating a competitive squad capable of challenging established Asian powers.
Their qualification campaign included victories over several traditional Asian football powers, establishing Jordan as a genuine force in the region.
Korea Republic: Korea Republic have appeared in the tournament 11 times consecutively since 1986, with their best finish being fourth place in 2002 as co-hosts.

The 2002 run to the semifinals was one of the most electrifying stories in tournament history, with dramatic victories over Spain and Italy thrilling millions of Korean fans in massive public viewings.
Korea Republic co-hosted the 2002 tournament with Japan, and the 'Red Devils' supporter movement became a global phenomenon during that edition.
Son Heung-min, the Tottenham Hotspur star, is the most prominent Korean player of the modern era and one of the most recognizable Asian footballers in the world.
In 2018, Korea Republic stunned defending champions Germany 2-0 in the group stage, contributing to Germany's shocking first-round elimination.
Mexico: Mexico have appeared in the tournament 18 times, making them one of the most frequent participants, with their best finish being the quarterfinals (1970, 1986).
Mexico hosted the tournament twice (1970 and 1986), with the 1986 edition being awarded to them after Colombia withdrew, and both tournaments produced iconic moments.
Mexico will co-host Football 2026 alongside the USA and Canada, making them the first country to host the tournament three times, with matches in Mexico City, Guadalajara, and Monterrey.
Hugo Sanchez, Rafael Marquez, and Javier 'Chicharito' Hernandez are among Mexico's greatest players to grace the tournament stage.
Mexico endured a streak of seven consecutive round-of-16 eliminations from 1994 to 2018, a pattern known as the 'quinto partido' curse, before failing to advance from the group in 2022.
Morocco: Morocco have appeared in the tournament 7 times, with their best finish being an extraordinary fourth place in 2022, when they became the first African and first Arab nation to reach the semifinals.
The 2022 run included victories over Belgium, Spain (on penalties), and Portugal, captivating fans worldwide and redefining expectations for African teams in the competition.
Morocco became the first African team to qualify for the tournament's second round in 1986, defeating Portugal in the group stage.
Achraf Hakimi's 'Panenka' penalty to eliminate Spain in the 2022 round of 16 became one of the most iconic moments of that tournament.
Morocco was the only African nation to bid for the 2026 edition of the tournament, losing the hosting rights to the joint USA-Canada-Mexico bid.
Netherlands: The Netherlands have appeared in the tournament 11 times and are the most successful team never to have won it, finishing as runners-up three times (1974, 1978, 2010).
The 1974 squad, led by Johan Cruyff, revolutionized football with 'Total Football,' a style of play that influenced the sport for generations despite losing the final to West Germany.
Johan Cruyff's famous 'Cruyff Turn' against Sweden in the 1974 tournament remains one of the most iconic individual moments in football history.
Dennis Bergkamp's last-minute wonder goal against Argentina in the 1998 quarterfinals is widely considered one of the greatest goals ever scored in the competition.
The Netherlands have produced an extraordinary lineage of talent including Marco van Basten, Ruud Gullit, Frank Rijkaard, Arjen Robben, and Robin van Persie.
New Zealand: New Zealand have appeared in the tournament 3 times (1982, 2010, 2026), with their 2010 squad famously remaining unbeaten with three draws in the group stage.

The All Whites' three draws in 2010 against Slovakia, Italy, and Paraguay meant they were one of only two unbeaten teams at that tournament (along with eventual champions Spain), though they did not advance.
New Zealand are typically the strongest team in Oceanian qualifying but face the challenge of an intercontinental playoff to reach the tournament.
Wynton Rufer, regarded as Oceania's greatest-ever player, represented New Zealand in their 1982 tournament debut in Spain.
Norway: Norway have appeared in the tournament 3 times (1938, 1994, 1998), with their best finish being the round of 16 in 1998.
In 1998, Norway defeated Brazil 2-1 in the group stage, one of the most memorable upsets of that tournament, with Kjetil Rekdal scoring the winner from the penalty spot.
Norway's 1936 Olympic football team famously defeated Germany in Berlin in front of Adolf Hitler, one of the most politically charged matches in football history.
Despite being a traditional winter sports powerhouse, Norway's return to Football 2026 after a 28-year absence marks a significant moment for Scandinavian football.
Panama: Panama have appeared in the tournament 3 times (2018, 2022, 2026), with their debut in 2018 being one of the most emotional qualification stories in CONCACAF history.
Roman Torres scored the dramatic stoppage-time goal against Costa Rica in October 2017 that sent Panama to their first tournament, sparking nationwide celebrations and a public holiday.
In their 2018 debut, Panama's Felipe Baloy scored the country's first-ever tournament goal in a 6-1 loss to England, a moment celebrated passionately by Panamanian fans.
Panama's continued presence in the tournament reflects the growing competitiveness of Central American football on the global stage.
Paraguay: Paraguay have appeared in the tournament 9 times, with their best finish being the quarterfinals in 2010, where they lost to Spain.
Goalkeeper Jose Luis Chilavert was one of the most colorful characters in tournament history, known for scoring free kicks and penalties from his own goal area.
Paraguay reached the round of 16 in both 1998 and 2002 with a disciplined defensive style that frustrated more fancied opponents.
Their 2010 run to the quarterfinals, where they defeated Japan on penalties in the round of 16, remains the high point of Paraguayan football in the competition.
Portugal: Portugal have appeared in the tournament 9 times, with their best finish being third place in 1966, when Eusebio won the Golden Boot with 9 goals.
Cristiano Ronaldo, the all-time leading international goal scorer, appeared in five consecutive tournaments (2006-2022) and scored 8 tournament goals across those editions.
Eusebio's brilliant performances in 1966, particularly his four goals against North Korea after Portugal trailed 3-0, remain among the greatest individual displays in tournament history.
Portugal reached the semifinals in 2006 under Luiz Felipe Scolari but have often been considered underachievers relative to their talent pool in the competition.
The 'Golden Generation' of Luis Figo, Rui Costa, and Joao Pinto was unable to replicate their success in the tournament despite being among the most gifted squads of their era.
Qatar: Qatar have appeared in the tournament twice (2022, 2026), hosting the event in 2022 in what was the first tournament held in the Middle East.
As hosts in 2022, Qatar became the first host nation to lose the opening match of the tournament, falling 2-0 to Ecuador, and were eliminated in the group stage.
Qatar's hosting of the 2022 event was historic as the first tournament held in November-December rather than the traditional June-July window.
Qatar's investment in football infrastructure and development, including the Aspire Academy, has produced a squad that won the 2019 Asian Cup despite having no prior tournament experience.
Saudi Arabia: Saudi Arabia have appeared in the tournament 7 times (1994, 1998, 2002, 2006, 2022, 2026), with their best finish being the round of 16 in 1994.
In 2022, Saudi Arabia produced one of the greatest upsets in tournament history by defeating Argentina 2-1, ending the South Americans' 36-match unbeaten run.
Saeed Al-Owairan's incredible solo goal against Belgium in 1994, dribbling past five defenders, is one of the most celebrated goals in tournament history.
Saudi Arabia's 1994 debut saw them reach the round of 16 in their first attempt, a strong debut that set high expectations for future editions.
Scotland: Scotland have appeared in the tournament 9 times (1954, 1958, 1974, 1978, 1982, 1986, 1990, 1998, 2026), but have never advanced beyond the group stage.
Scotland's inability to progress past the first round despite 8 previous attempts makes their Football 2026 appearance a chance to finally break through after a 28-year absence from the event.
The 1978 campaign in Argentina was marked by chaos and controversy, though Archie Gemmill's stunning solo goal against the Netherlands remains one of the great tournament goals.
Kenny Dalglish, Denis Law, and Jim Baxter are among Scotland's most famous footballers, though none were able to lead the nation past the group stage.
Senegal: Senegal have appeared in the tournament 4 times (2002, 2018, 2022, 2026), with their best finish being the quarterfinals in their stunning 2002 debut.
In 2002, Senegal shocked the world by defeating defending champions France 1-0 in the opening match, with Papa Bouba Diop scoring the winner in one of the tournament's greatest upsets.
Sadio Mane, the 2022 African Footballer of the Year, has been the face of Senegalese football and led the Lions of Teranga in both the 2018 and 2022 tournaments.
Senegal won their first-ever Africa Cup of Nations title in 2022, establishing themselves as one of the strongest African nations in modern football.
South Africa: South Africa have appeared in the tournament 4 times (1998, 2002, 2010, 2026), and hosted the 2010 edition, becoming the first African nation to do so.
The 2010 tournament opened with the iconic sound of vuvuzelas and Siphiwe Tshabalala's spectacular opening goal against Mexico, one of the most memorable moments in tournament history.
South Africa remain the only host nation to be eliminated in the group stage of the tournament, failing to advance in 2010 despite a victory over France.
Bafana Bafana's participation in the 1998 tournament, led by coach Clive Barker, marked their return to international football after decades of apartheid-era isolation.
Spain: Spain have appeared in the tournament 17 times and won it once, in 2010, when they defeated the Netherlands 1-0 in the final with Andres Iniesta's iconic extra-time goal.
The 2010 triumph was the centerpiece of an unprecedented era of dominance in which Spain also won the 2008 and 2012 European Championships with their revolutionary tiki-taka possession style.
Spain hosted the tournament in 1982, with 24 teams participating for the first time, though the hosts were eliminated in the second group stage.
Xavi, Iniesta, and Sergio Busquets formed one of the greatest midfield trios in football history, controlling matches with an approach that redefined how the sport was played.
Fernando Torres, David Villa, and Raul are among Spain's top tournament scorers, with Villa netting 5 goals during the victorious 2010 campaign.
Sweden: Sweden have appeared in the tournament 13 times, with their best finish being runners-up in 1958 when they hosted the event and lost to Brazil in the final.
The 1958 tournament, hosted by Sweden, is best remembered for introducing a 17-year-old Pele to the world stage, though Sweden's run to the final was a remarkable achievement.
Sweden finished third in 1950 and 1994, with the 1994 squad featuring the prolific partnership of Kennet Andersson, Martin Dahlin, and Tomas Brolin.
Zlatan Ibrahimovic, despite never scoring in the tournament itself, is Sweden's most famous modern player and led their qualifying campaigns with his trademark flair and personality.
Switzerland: Switzerland have appeared in the tournament 12 times, with their best finish being the quarterfinals in 1934, 1938, and 1954.
Switzerland hosted the 1954 tournament, which featured the 'Miracle of Bern' final in which West Germany came from 2-0 down to defeat Hungary 3-2.
Xherdan Shaqiri scored one of the most spectacular goals in recent tournament history, an overhead bicycle kick against Poland in the 2018 round of 16.
Switzerland have been consistent qualifiers in the modern era, reaching the knockout stage in 2006, 2014, 2018, and 2022, but have struggled to break through to the semifinals.
Tunisia: Tunisia have appeared in the tournament 7 times (1978, 1998, 2002, 2006, 2018, 2022, 2026), making them one of Africa's most frequent participants.
In 1978, Tunisia became the first African team to win a match in the tournament, defeating Mexico 3-1, a landmark moment for African football.
Tunisia have never advanced beyond the group stage, though they came close in 2022 with a famous victory over France in their final group match.
The Eagles of Carthage are known for their passionate fan support, with Tunisian supporters creating vibrant atmospheres at every tournament they attend.
Türkiye: Türkiye have appeared in the tournament 3 times (1954, 2002, 2026), with their best finish being third place in 2002.
The 2002 tournament was a golden era for Turkish football, as Hakan Sukur scored the fastest goal in tournament history (10.89 seconds) in the third-place match against Korea Republic.
Türkiye's run in 2002 included stunning victories over co-hosts Japan in the round of 16 and Senegal in the quarterfinals, thrilling fans around the world.
Their Football 2026 appearance ends a 24-year absence from the competition and marks only their third time qualifying for the event.
USA: The USA have appeared in the tournament 12 times, with their best finish being third place in the inaugural 1930 edition and the quarterfinals in 2002.
The USA will co-host Football 2026 alongside Canada and Mexico, with matches in 11 cities across the country including the opening match and final at MetLife Stadium in New Jersey.
The USA's 1-0 victory over England in the 1950 tournament is considered one of the greatest upsets in the history of the sport, achieved by a squad of part-time players.
Landon Donovan's stoppage-time goal against Algeria in 2010 is one of the most iconic moments in American sports history, sending the USA through to the round of 16.
Christian Pulisic, nicknamed 'Captain America,' has emerged as the face of American football and will lead the team on home soil in Football 2026.
Uruguay: Uruguay have appeared in the tournament 14 times and won it twice (1930, 1950), with a fourth-place finish in 2010 being their best modern result.
Uruguay hosted and won the inaugural tournament in 1930, defeating Argentina 4-2 in the final in Montevideo, making them the first-ever champions.
The 'Maracanazo' of 1950, when Uruguay defeated Brazil 2-1 in the de facto final at the Maracana in front of nearly 200,000 spectators, is one of the most legendary moments in sports history.
Diego Forlan won the Golden Ball as the best player of the 2010 tournament, leading Uruguay to the semifinals with a series of spectacular long-range goals.
Luis Suarez is Uruguay's all-time leading scorer and is remembered for both his brilliance and his controversies, including the deliberate handball against Ghana in the 2010 quarterfinals.
Uzbekistan: Uzbekistan's qualification for Football 2026 marks their first-ever appearance in the tournament, a historic achievement for Central Asian football.
Uzbekistan have been a consistent force in Asian football, regularly reaching the knockout stages of the Asian Cup, but had never managed to qualify for the tournament until now.
The White Wolves' qualification ended decades of near-misses, having come agonizingly close in several previous qualifying campaigns, particularly in 2006 and 2018.
Server Djeparov, twice named Asian Footballer of the Year, is the most celebrated Uzbek player in history, though he was unable to lead the team to a tournament during his career.
